content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You’ve been told a hundred times: “Always add a key prop when rendering lists." So you slap key={index} on your .map(), the warning disappears, and you move on.
Then one day, a checkbox you toggled shows up checked on the wrong item after you delete a row. Or a text input loses focus while you’re typing. Or an animation glitches on reorder. You didn’t touch that logic — so what happened?
The warning didn’t lie to you. It just didn’t tell you the whole story. Keys aren’t a checkbox-warning silencer — they’re how React figures out which thing is which across renders. Get that part wrong, and the bugs it causes look nothing like a “missing key” problem.
What a Key Actually Is
A key is a hint you give React: “this element represents the same conceptual thing across renders, even if its position or content changes.”
{users.map(user => (
<UserCard key={user.id} name={user.name} />
))}That’s it. Keys aren’t passed to your component as props, they’re not for styling, and they’re not a general-purpose unique ID for your own logic. Their entire job is to help React’s reconciler track identity.
Why Reconciliation Needs Identity
When state changes, React doesn’t diff the DOM against the DOM. It diffs the new element tree against the previous element tree, then applies the minimal set of changes to the real DOM.
For a single element, this is straightforward — same type, same spot, update it. But for a list, React has a harder problem: if the array goes from [A, B, C] to [B, C, A], are these the same three items rearranged, or three brand-new items?
Without keys, React’s default heuristic is positional: it compares index 0 to index 0, index 1 to index 1, and so on. It has no concept of “the item that used to be first is now last” — it just sees “the thing at position 0 changed from A to B” and updates it in place.
Keys give React the missing signal. With stable keys, React can match B (position 1 before) to B (position 0 now) and understand: this didn't change, it just moved. No re-creation, no lost state — just a reorder.
How This Affects Performance and State
Two things happen when React correctly matches an old element to a new one via its key:

• Performance: React reuses the existing DOM node and component instance instead of destroying and recreating them. For a list of 500 rows, that’s the difference between one DOM move and 500 fresh mounts.

• State preservation: Each component instance keeps its own internal state (useState, uncontrolled input values, CSS transition state, scroll position) only if React believes it's the same instance. That belief is entirely driven by the key.
This is the part people underestimate. It’s not just about speed — it’s about correctness of state. If React mismatches identity, it doesn’t crash. It silently attaches the wrong internal state to the wrong visual row. That’s far more dangerous than a slow render, because nothing tells you it happened.
Why Array Index as a Key Goes Wrong
// Looks fine until the list changes shape
{todos.map((todo, index) => (
<TodoItem key={index} text={todo.text} />
))}This works only while the list is append-only and never reorders or deletes from the middle. The moment you remove todos[0], everything shifts up by one index. React sees "index 0 changed content" for every row below the deletion — not "one item was removed." Each TodoItem now thinks it's a different todo, but keeps its old component state (like an open "editing" toggle or an uncontrolled input's cursor position), because from React's point of view, the component instance at that index never left.
Index keys are acceptable when all of the following are true:

• The list is static and never reorders.

• Items are never inserted or removed from the middle.

• The list has no per-item local state or uncontrolled inputs.
In practice, that’s mostly static, read-only lists — which is a narrower case than most codebases actually have.
Common Mistakes

• Using Math.random() or Date.now() as a key. This generates a new key on every render, so React treats every item as brand new every single time — you get the worst case of index keys, always. Never do this.

• Keying by array index “to make the warning go away.” Silences the linter, doesn’t fix the identity problem.

• Assuming keys need to be globally unique. They only need to be unique among siblings in the same list — not across your whole app.

• Putting the key on the wrong element when a list item is a fragment or wrapper — the key belongs on the outermost element returned for each iteration.

• Forgetting keys reset state on purpose, sometimes usefully. Changing a key intentionally (e.g., key={formId}) is a valid pattern to force React to remount a component and wipe its state — useful for resetting a form when switching between records.
Key Takeaways

• Keys tell React which element is “the same one” across renders — they’re an identity signal, not a styling or logic prop.

• Reconciliation without good keys falls back to positional matching, which breaks down the moment lists reorder, insert, or delete from the middle.

• Correct keys let React reuse DOM nodes and component instances, preserving both performance and internal state.

• Bad keys don’t crash your app — they silently attach the wrong state to the wrong row, which is harder to debug than a visible error.

• Use a stable, unique-per-sibling identifier (usually a database ID) as your default. Reach for the array index only for lists that are static, non-reorderable, and stateless per item.
